title: Daily Temperatures
code: var ans = new int[n];                      // defaults to 0
var st = new Stack<int>();                 // decreasing stack of indices
ans[j] = i - j;                    // days waited for a warmer temp

frames.caption: Set up ans[] (all 0) and an empty decreasing stack of indices, then scan left to right.
frames.note: ans starts all zeros, which doubles as the default "no warmer day ever comes." The stack will hold the indices of days still waiting for a warmer day, kept in decreasing temperature order from bottom to top. One linear pass fills every answer — O(n) time, O(n) space.

frames.caption: Day 0 (temp 73): check whether it resolves any waiting day on the stack.
frames.note: For each day we first ask: is today warmer than the day currently on top of the stack? If so, that day has found its warmer future day and we can fill in its answer. The decreasing-stack invariant means the top is always the most-recently-waiting (and coolest) unresolved day.

frames.caption: Stack is empty → no waiting day to resolve for day 0.
frames.note: Day i is not warmer than the day on top, so no waiting day is resolved yet. The top stays unanswered for now — it is still hoping for a warmer day further right — and day i simply gets pushed on top of it.

frames.caption: Push day 0 onto the stack — it now waits for a warmer day.
frames.note: After resolving everyone day i is warmer than, day i itself joins the line of days still waiting for a warmer future day. Pushing it keeps the stack decreasing by temperature (every day below it is warmer), which is the invariant that makes the resolve-on-pop logic correct.

frames.caption: Day 1 (temp 74): check whether it resolves any waiting day on the stack.
frames.note: For each day we first ask: is today warmer than the day currently on top of the stack? If so, that day has found its warmer future day and we can fill in its answer. The decreasing-stack invariant means the top is always the most-recently-waiting (and coolest) unresolved day.

frames.caption: temperatures[1]=74 > temperatures[0]=73 → day 0 resolves on pop.
frames.note: The while condition holds: today is strictly warmer than the day on top. That top day has been waiting and day i is its answer, so we drop into the loop body to pop it and record the distance.

frames.caption: Pop day 0; ans[0] = 1 - 0 = 1 (days waited).
frames.note: This is the heart of the monotonic stack: the day on top has been waiting for a warmer temperature, and day i is the first one warmer than it. So its answer is known *now* — ans[j] = i - j days waited. We keep popping because day i may be warmer than several waiting days at once (the stack is decreasing, so once i beats the top it may beat the ones below too).

frames.caption: Push day 1 onto the stack — it now waits for a warmer day.
frames.note: After resolving everyone day i is warmer than, day i itself joins the line of days still waiting for a warmer future day. Pushing it keeps the stack decreasing by temperature (every day below it is warmer), which is the invariant that makes the resolve-on-pop logic correct.

frames.caption: temperatures[2]=75 > temperatures[1]=74 → day 1 resolves on pop.
frames.note: The while condition holds: today is strictly warmer than the day on top. That top day has been waiting and day i is its answer, so we drop into the loop body to pop it and record the distance.

frames.caption: Pop day 1; ans[1] = 2 - 1 = 1 (days waited).
frames.note: This is the heart of the monotonic stack: the day on top has been waiting for a warmer temperature, and day i is the first one warmer than it. So its answer is known *now* — ans[j] = i - j days waited. We keep popping because day i may be warmer than several waiting days at once (the stack is decreasing, so once i beats the top it may beat the ones below too).

frames.caption: temperatures[7]=73 ≤ temperatures[6]=76 → nothing to resolve.
frames.note: Day i is not warmer than the day on top, so no waiting day is resolved yet. The top stays unanswered for now — it is still hoping for a warmer day further right — and day i simply gets pushed on top of it.

frames.caption: Scan complete → ans = [1, 1, 4, 2, 1, 1, 0, 0]. Days still on the stack never warmed and stay 0.
frames.note: The two indices left on the stack (days 6 and 7, temps 76 and 73) never met a warmer day to their right, so their answers remain the default 0. Every index was pushed once and popped at most once, confirming the amortized O(n) bound even with the nested while loop.
